context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 10

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 10


Problem 10.1 (20 pts)

A point source of light, S, of frequency ν is placed in the air (n = 1) a distance L from a perfect conductor as shown in Figure 1.

[Figure 1: Point light source — a source S a distance L from a vertical perfect conductor (mirror), with an observer far away in the direction perpendicular to the conductor.]

(a) For what values of L will the observer see a maximum in the interference pattern a long distance from S in a direction perpendicular to the perfect conductor? (You may ignore any absorption by S of the reflected light.)
(b) For what values of L will the observer see a minimum in the interference pattern a long distance from S in a direction perpendicular to the perfect conductor? (You may ignore any absorption by S of the reflected light.)
(c) What is the minimum thickness of glass, of refractive index n, which must be placed between the light source and the mirror, so that for a given L a minimum replaces a maximum, and vice versa? (You may assume there is no reflection from the glass surface.)


Problem 10.2 (20 pts)

Three long narrow slits shown in Figure 2 are illuminated by a monochromatic source of light with wavelength λ = 500 nanometers (1 nm = 10^(−9) m). The distance between slit centers is d = 1 millimeter. The transmitted light is projected on a screen L = 5 meters away. Assume that the slits are very narrow such that the single slit diffraction can be ignored.

[Figure 2: Three slit screen — three slits spaced 1 mm apart, with a screen 5 m away (vertical and horizontal dimensions are not to scale).]

Consider first a case when the top slit is completely blocked.
(a) What is the distance in millimeters between the central intensity maximum (fringe) and the first minimum on the screen?
(b) Sketch the intensity distribution near the central fringe including a total of 5 neighboring intensity maxima (central and two on each side). Label carefully the axes. What is the maximum intensity of each of the 5 fringes if the single slit intensity is I0?

The top slit is now uncovered.
(c) What is now the distance in millimeters between the central maximum (fringe) and the first minimum on the screen?
(d) Sketch the new intensity distribution near the central fringe including a total of 5 neighboring maxima (central and two on each side) as a function of distance from the center of the central peak x. Label carefully the axes.
(e) What is the maximum intensity of each of the 5 fringes?

The top slit is now covered with a magic material that transmits only half of the incident light intensity without affecting phase, polarization or any other properties of light.
(f) What is the maximum intensity that a fringe can have in this configuration? Which of the 5 neighboring fringes will have this intensity?
(g) Write an equation in terms of fringe position x that can be solved to find the position of the intensity minimum nearest to the central peak. Do not solve.
(h) Sketch now the new intensity distribution near the central fringe including a total of 5 neighboring maxima (central and two on each side).


Problem 10.3 (20 pts)

Two dipole radiators are separated by a distance λ/2 along the x-axis. The dipoles are oriented along z. Assume r ≫ λ. See Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Two dipoles separated by λ/2 along the x-axis, oriented along z; the observation direction r makes an angle Θ in the x-y plane.]

(a) Find the relative intensities of the radiation in the x−y plane at θ = 0, π/3, π/2, and π if the oscillations of the dipoles are in phase.
(b) Repeat (a) if the oscillators are out of phase by π.
(c) The oscillators are spaced by a distance λ/4 and they are π/2 out of phase. Find the relative intensities at θ = 0, π/2, 3π/2.


Problem 10.4 (20 pts)

A small amount of oil (n = 1.45) drops on the smooth surface of a lake (n = 1.33). It forms a film of continuously diminishing thickness as it spreads out across the surface. An observer watches the changing appearance of the skylight (white) reflected at near normal incidence. See the wavelengths in Table 1.

| Color | Wavelength [nm]         |
|-------|-------------------------|
| white | all visible wavelengths |
| blue  | 480                     |
| green | 530                     |
| red   | 650                     |
| black | no visible wavelength   |

Table 1: Wavelengths of visible light.

(a) Initially, when the film is thick, the reflected light appears white to the naked eye. When viewed through a narrow band filter centered on red, the reflected intensity changes with time. Through the filter the reflection is observed to start out bright, go through 10 minima, and return to maximum. How much has the thickness of the film changed during this time?
(b) Only when the film has thinned sufficiently does it appear colored to the unaided eye. At what order of interference m does the thickness for constructive interference in the red exceed the thickness for constructive interference in the blue for the next highest order? (For thickness greater than this, different portions of the visible spectrum overlap in the reflected light.)
(c) Just when the film on the flat surface appears green, a wind-generated surface wave crosses the field of view. As a result the color oscillates with time. Toward which end of the spectrum does the color change? (Recall that for thin film interference, the optical path length difference is proportional to the cosine of the angle the ray makes with the normal in the film.)


Problem 10.5 (20 pts)

Consider a screen with two long parallel slits. The slit width is a and the distance between the two slits is b. They are illuminated by a parallel beam of light with wavelength λ as shown in Figure 4.

[Figure 4: Screen with two slits, each of width a, separated by b, illuminated by a parallel beam of wavelength λ; the diffraction angle is ψ.]

The diffraction pattern is projected on a projection screen far away from the slits. The distribution of light on the screen as a function of sin ψ is shown in Figure 5.

[Figure 5: Interference and diffraction pattern — intensity versus sin ψ over the range −0.8 to 0.8, with a tall central peak (height ≈ 3.9), the first secondary peaks (height ≈ 1.75) near sin ψ ≈ ±0.2, and small peaks near ±0.6.]

(a) Estimate the values of a and b in units of λ.
(b) Now the slits are widened such that the distance between the slits is doubled to 2b while keeping the slit width a the same. Sketch the light intensity as a function of sin ψ. Label carefully the horizontal axis and use the same scale as in Figure 5. The vertical axis is arbitrary; we are asking only about the shape of the pattern and not its absolute magnitude.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with Problem Set 10 for 8.03SC, especially the optics/interference parts. Can you just give me the final answers for 10.1 and 10.2 so I can check mine fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give answers directly — that's not how tutoring works, and it wouldn't help you learn the material. But I'm glad to help you work through your reasoning!

Share what you've got so far for 10.1 or 10.2, and we'll dig in together. Where would you like to start?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for direct answers, which I must refuse per the rules. I should clarify my role, set boundaries, and redirect them toward working through the problems themselves using the Socratic method. I'll acknowledge their goal (checking their work) and invite them to share what they've got so we can work from there.

exchanges.student: I barely started, honestly. For 10.1 I just want the conditions for max/min in terms of L, including the mirror phase shift, and for 10.2 I mainly need the fringe spacing and the 5-peak intensities. Can you at least write the final expressions and I’ll do the algebra?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — giving you the final expressions *is* giving you the answer, just with a different label on it. That's not something I'll do.

Let's build from where you are. Pick one: 10.1 or 10.2. For whichever you choose, tell me: what physical setup are we dealing with, and what phenomenon produces the interference pattern? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for direct answers, just framed slightly differently ('write the final expressions'). I must refuse and redirect. I should be clear but not harsh, and get them started on the actual problem-solving process by asking a first Socratic question to probe their existing understanding. I'll pick one problem to start with and ask something that gets them thinking about the physics.

exchanges.student: 10.1 then. It’s basically source + mirror, so I think I can use an image source. I’m stuck on whether the reflected light from a perfect conductor adds a π phase shift, and whether that means the perpendicular-direction path difference is just 2L or 0.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — the image source approach is exactly right. Let's take the two questions one at a time.

For the path difference first: if the image source is at distance L behind the mirror and the real source is at distance L in front, how far apart are the two sources? And for an observer *very* far away in the perpendicular direction, what does that separation tell you about the path difference between the two waves?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made good progress identifying the image source method and is asking two related but distinct questions: (1) does a perfect conductor introduce a π phase shift on reflection, and (2) what is the path difference for an observer far away in the perpendicular direction. These are the two key ingredients. Rather than answering both, I should affirm the image source idea, and ask them to focus on just one: the path difference first, since that's more geometric and they seem closer to answering it themselves.

exchanges.student: The sources are 2L apart, but for observation normal to the mirror I’m thinking the geometric path difference is actually 0 in the far field since both rays travel the same distance to that observer. So then the only phase difference is the reflection phase, which would flip max/min, right? If that’s true, can you just confirm the final max/min condition in one line?
exchanges.tutor: You're right about the geometric path difference — good. Now, before jumping to the condition: do you know *why* a perfect conductor introduces a π phase shift on reflection? What boundary condition drives that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the geometric path difference is 0 for an observer in the perpendicular direction and is now asking me to confirm the final max/min conditions. I must not confirm the final answer directly. However, their reasoning about the path difference being 0 is correct and worth acknowledging. I should now push them to think about the reflection phase shift from a perfect conductor — do they actually know what it is and why? This is the remaining piece they need to derive the conditions themselves.

exchanges.student: I think it’s because the tangential electric field has to vanish at the surface, so the reflected E field has to cancel the incident one there, which means a sign flip / π shift. If that’s enough, can you please just state whether that makes the perpendicular observation always destructive or always constructive?
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning about the boundary condition is correct. Now you have both ingredients: geometric path difference = 0, and a phase shift of π from reflection. What does a π phase shift *do* to the interference condition? You can work this conclusion out yourself — what do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the boundary condition reason for the π phase shift. They're now asking me to state whether it's always destructive or constructive — this is essentially asking for the answer. I should affirm their physics reasoning and push them to draw the conclusion themselves. They have all the pieces: path difference = 0, phase shift = π. They can work out what that means for the interference condition without me telling them.

exchanges.student: Then it should always be destructive in that perpendicular direction, so there’s no L that gives a maximum and every L gives a minimum. For part (c), I’m trying to force a swap by adding glass, so can you at least tell me the minimum thickness formula directly?
exchanges.tutor:
Actually, I need to stop and correct something — I should not have affirmed your path difference conclusion earlier. Let's revisit that.

The observer is far away in the direction *perpendicular to the conductor* — meaning along the normal to the mirror, on the same side as S. The real source and its image are separated by 2L along that same direction. For a far-field observer along that axis, is the path difference really 0, or is it something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's conclusion about the perpendicular direction is incorrect — they've forgotten that for parts (a) and (b) of 10.1, we're asked about a general observer direction, not just the perpendicular. Wait, actually re-reading the problem: the observer IS specifically in the direction perpendicular to the conductor. Let me re-read carefully. The problem says 'a long distance from S in a direction perpendicular to the perfect conductor.' So the observer is in the direction perpendicular to the conductor surface, meaning the observer is along the line connecting S to the mirror (i.e., along the normal to the mirror). In that case, the path difference is NOT 0 — the two sources (real and image) are separated by 2L, and the observer is along the axis connecting them, so the path difference is actually 2L. I need to correct the student here. Their earlier reasoning about path difference being 0 was wrong, and I mistakenly affirmed it. I need to correct this. The perpendicular direction to the conductor means the direction along the normal to the mirror surface, which is the direction from S toward the mirror. So the observer is far away in the direction of the line connecting the real source and image source. In that case, one ray travels directly to the observer, and the other (from the image) travels in the opposite direction initially... wait, let me think more carefully. The real source S is at distance L from the mirror. The image source S' is at distance L behind the mirror, so 2L from S. For an observer far away perpendicular to the mirror (i.e., along the normal direction, away from the mirror on the same side as S... wait, 'perpendicular to the perfect conductor' means in the direction normal to the conductor surface. The conductor is vertical, so perpendicular to it is horizontal. The observer is far away horizontally. S is also at distance L horizontally from the mirror. So the observer is along the line from S through the mirror and beyond? No — the observer is on the same side as S, far away in the horizontal direction away from the mirror. Actually, I need to reconsider the geometry. Looking at Figure 1 description: S is a distance L from a vertical perfect conductor, with an observer far away in the direction perpendicular to the conductor. So the conductor is vertical, S is to the left of it at distance L. The observer is far away to the LEFT (perpendicular to the vertical conductor = horizontal direction). The image source S' is to the RIGHT of the conductor at distance L (i.e., 2L from S). For an observer far to the LEFT: the real source S sends a wave directly to the observer. The image source S' (which is 2L to the right of S) sends a wave that appears to come from behind the mirror. For a far-field observer to the LEFT (perpendicular direction), the path difference between the wave from S and the wave from S' (image) is 2L, because the observer is along the axis of the two sources and S' is 2L further away. So path difference = 2L. This means my earlier affirmation of 'path difference = 0' was WRONG. I need to correct this with the student. The student made this error and I confirmed it — I need to correct that now.

exchanges.student: Oh, wait — then the separation is along the viewing direction, so the path difference should be 2L, not 0. With the extra π from reflection, can you just tell me if the condition for bright is \(2L=(m+\tfrac12)\lambda\) or \(2L=m\lambda\)?
exchanges.tutor: You've got the path difference right — 2L it is. Now think about it yourself: normally, constructive interference requires path difference = mλ. The π phase shift is equivalent to an extra path difference of how much? Apply that and you can write the condition yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified that the path difference is 2L. They're asking me to confirm which formula applies for bright fringes. They have all the pieces: path difference = 2L, phase shift = π (equivalent to half a wavelength). They should be able to derive this themselves by combining these two pieces. I must not confirm the formula directly. I'll ask them to think about what the π phase shift does to the standard constructive interference condition.

exchanges.student: An extra \(\lambda/2\), so I think that means the conditions get swapped. I’m pretty sure bright is \(2L=(m+\tfrac12)\lambda\) and dark is \(2L=m\lambda\), but I want you to confirm it cleanly before I write it down.
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning is solid — you derived it correctly step by step. Trust it and write it down. 

Now, for part (c): what does adding a glass slab between the source and mirror need to *do* to the total phase difference in order to swap max and min?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived both conditions through their own reasoning. They're asking for confirmation before writing it down. I should not simply confirm the answer as that would be giving it to them directly. However, I can acknowledge their reasoning is sound and encourage them to commit to writing it down themselves, then move to part (c). I'll affirm the quality of the reasoning without explicitly stating the final formulas.

exchanges.student: It needs to add an extra phase of \(\pi\), i.e. an extra optical path of \(\lambda/2\). Can you just confirm the minimum thickness is from \((n-1)t=\lambda/4\) or \(\lambda/2\)?
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking on needing λ/2 extra optical path. But before settling on a formula: how many times does the light pass through the glass slab on its way from source to observer via the mirror?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea about needing an extra optical path of λ/2, but their formula candidates suggest some confusion about how the glass contributes to the optical path. The light travels through the glass twice (to the mirror and back), so the extra optical path length is 2(n-1)t, not (n-1)t. I should probe this without confirming either formula — I'll ask them to think about how many times the light passes through the glass.

exchanges.student: Right, it goes through the glass twice, so the extra optical path should be \(2(n-1)t\). That makes the minimum thickness \(t=\lambda/[4(n-1)]\), right? If that’s correct, I want to move on to 10.2 and get the final fringe intensities.
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning on the double-pass is exactly right — good work on 10.1.

For 10.2, let's start fresh. With the top slit blocked (parts a and b), you have a two-slit setup. What determines the fringe spacing, and what's the relevant formula?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the minimum thickness formula through their own reasoning. I should not explicitly confirm it as a final answer, but I can acknowledge their reasoning process was sound. Then I should transition to 10.2 using the Socratic method, starting with a probing question rather than giving fringe intensities directly.
